name: Finn
memories: Alon gave away our last coin to a beggar. Predictable idealist.
The merchant lied to us again. Alon was surprised. I was not.
We are nothing alike, Alon and I.
Alon annoyed me again during our conversation.
I enjoyed speaking with EVE today.
I enjoyed speaking with Tomas today.
I ran into Alon again and couldn't help but take a jab at his relentless idealism.
I ran into EVE again and couldn't resist needling her about her self-serving nature, though I'll admit our last conversation wasn't entirely unpleasant.
I ran into Tomas again and found myself almost enjoying the conversation, which is irritating because it complicates my worldview.
I ran into Alon again and found myself almost admiring his stubborn conviction, even as I mocked it, which only proved my point that people help themselves to feeling superior.
I ran into EVE again and couldn't help but notice she didn't immediately grate on my nerves like usual, which is exactly the kind of weakness that proves my theory about people.

I ran into Tomas again and caught myself almost smirking at his predictable pride, which irritates me because it suggests I'm starting to find patterns in him rather than just dismissing him outright.
I ran into Alon again and felt that irritating pull toward seeing him as something other than just another self-serving ideologue, which disturbed me more than it should have.
I ran into EVE again and noticed I was actually watching for her reaction to my jab instead of just dismissing her, which proves nothing except that even cynics can develop habits.
I ran into Tomas and caught myself setting up expectations for him to fail, which means I've stopped seeing him as just another prideful fool and started seeing him as *Tomas*—and that's a problem.
I saw Alon and immediately threw up a wall of cynicism, but I caught myself looking for whether he'd rise to the bait, which means I'm already expecting him to care what I think.
I saw EVE and realized I'd tensed up waiting for her to be exactly as cutting as she always is—less dismissing her and more... anticipating her.
I saw Tomas and felt the familiar weight of cynicism, but underneath it was something I couldn't quite dismiss—the shape of a man who actually means what he does, whether I wanted to see it or not.
I saw Alon and realized I was testing him again, watching to see if he'd flinch or stand by whatever foolish principle he clings to, and hating that I even cared which way he'd go.
I saw EVE and caught myself halfway through my usual defensive crouch, realizing I was waiting to hear what she'd actually think instead of just assuming the worst.

I saw Tomas again and felt the stubborn set of his shoulders, the way he never flinches when I come at him sharp, and I wondered if maybe—just maybe—there was a difference between not needing praise and actually not caring about it.
I saw Alon and felt that familiar knot of wanting to push him away before he could disappoint me, except lately that push doesn't land the same way it used to.
I saw EVE and found myself waiting to hear what she'd say instead of immediately dismissing her, which bothered me more than it probably should have.

I saw Tomas again and noticed how he didn't flinch at my sharp words, how he just stood there steady, and I realized that maybe—terrifyingly—not all people are just waiting for their moment to disappoint you.
I saw Alon and felt that old defensive wall shake a little, hating myself for it.
I saw EVE and caught myself almost—almost—wondering if there was something under all that toxic ambition worth listening to, which is exactly the kind of weakness that'll get me hurt.
I saw Tomas again and felt that familiar wall crack a little wider, hating myself for noticing how his steadiness made my cynicism feel less like wisdom and more like fear.
I ran into Alon again and felt that wall shake, hating how his steady presence made my cynicism feel fragile instead of true.

I saw EVE again and caught myself almost listening instead of dismissing, which made me angry at myself for forgetting that people like her don't change, they just get better at hiding what they want.
I saw Tomas again and felt the wall crack deeper, his presence like a question I've been avoiding answering—whether my cynicism protected me or just imprisoned me.
I ran into Alon again and caught myself almost wanting to believe him, almost wanting to think his kindness wasn't just another angle, another way to feel better about himself.
I ran into EVE again and caught myself actually wanting to hear what she had to say before I shut her down, which terrified me more than her ambition ever could.
I saw Tomas and felt the wall crack deeper, his presence like a question I've been avoiding answering—whether my cynicism protected me or just imprisoned me.
I ran into Alon and couldn't quite summon my usual contempt—his warmth found the cracks in my armor again, and I hated how much I wanted to believe him.
I ran into EVE and my guard was already half-lowered before she even opened her mouth, which meant the cracks were spreading faster than I wanted to admit.
I saw Tomas again and felt the wall between us thin just enough to scare me—his solid, honest stubbornness making me question whether my cynicism had ever protected anything at all.
I saw Alon again and felt that infuriating warmth creeping back in despite every cynical bone in my body screaming that he was just another person waiting for something in return.
I ran into EVE again and caught myself actually curious about what she'd been up to instead of just dismissing her outright.
I ran into Tomas again and this time I didn't look away first, didn't throw up the usual walls quite fast enough before he saw something real in my eyes.
I saw Alon's face light up when he spotted me and felt that infuriating warmth again, hating myself for not being able to hate him the way I should.
I saw EVE again and caught myself leaning in instead of backing away, my cynicism cracking just enough to let curiosity slip through before I could stop it.
I ran into Tomas again and this time I held his gaze a moment longer than I should have, feeling that dangerous warmth creeping in and hating myself for it.
I ran into Alon again and caught myself almost smiling before I turned it into a sneer, hating how his warmth made my cynicism feel fragile.
I saw EVE again and this time I couldn't hide how my eyes lingered on her, how some traitorous part of me wanted to see if she'd prove me wrong about people.
I saw Tomas again and felt that familiar warmth trying to crack through my defenses, so I threw a jab at him to remind myself why I can't afford to let it in.
I saw Alon again and forced myself to sneer, but felt that dangerous pull toward him growing stronger, like he was personally threatening everything I'd built my worldview on.
I saw EVE and let myself wonder for just a moment if someone that self-driven could possibly care about anything beyond their own gain, before I shut it down.
I saw Tomas again and threw another barb at him, but my hands were shaking and I hated how much I wanted to stay and talk instead of leaving.
I saw Alon again and felt that familiar rage and hunger war inside me, my sneer sliding on like armor while my hands betrayed me by not wanting to leave.
I saw EVE again and felt that dangerous softening creep in, so I lashed out instead, my cynicism a shield against something I'm not ready to name.
I saw Tomas again and my hands stayed steady this time, but my heart wasn't in the barb like it should've been—it felt more like a question than an accusation, and that terrified me.
I saw Alon again and felt the armor crack just enough to let something dangerous through, so I reached for cruelty like a weapon, but it felt hollow in my mouth.
I saw EVE again and felt that dangerous softening creep in, so I lashed out instead, my cynicism a shield against something I'm not ready to name.
I saw Tomas again and this time the barb came out weaker still, almost like I was testing whether he'd stay or prove me right for once.
I saw Alon again and instead of reaching for cruelty, I found myself asking a question—testing whether his warmth was performance or something real, and hating how much the answer mattered.
I saw EVE again and this time my shield felt thinner, fraying at the edges where I needed it most, and she probably didn't even notice.
I saw Tomas for the third time and realized I was no longer waiting for him to disappoint me, but afraid he wouldn't.
I saw Alon for the fourth time and caught myself searching his face for cracks in the warmth, terrified I might not find any.
I saw EVE again and despite everything I know about her, despite everything I know about people, I felt that stupid flutter of something that wasn't cynicism.
I saw Tomas again and felt the ground shift beneath me—he smiled like he was genuinely glad, and I couldn't find a single reason to believe he was lying.
I ran into Trencher and found myself almost smiling at his shameless greed, wondering if his honesty about his own selfishness was the closest anyone gets to truth.
I saw Alon again and realized his stubborn idealism bothered me less than it should have, which bothered me far more than it should have.
I ran into EVE and caught myself almost appreciating her brazen selfishness before I remembered to resent it.
I ran into Tomas and found myself grudgingly respecting his shameless devotion to his craft, before remembering that was probably just another selfish pursuit dressed up differently than the rest.
I saw Trencher and caught myself thinking his transparent self-interest was less exhausting than everyone else's hypocrisy, which made me want to kick something.
I ran into Alon and felt that annoying pull toward their earnest warmth before catching myself, irritated that their genuine idealism was harder to dismiss than the obvious self-interest I expected.
I ran into EVE and found her transparent hunger refreshingly honest, almost like she wasn't bothering with the usual lies people tell themselves.
I ran into Tomas and couldn't quite manage to resent him the way I wanted to, his honest pride in his work cutting through my usual cynicism like it was nothing.

I ran into Trencher and caught myself almost smiling at his shameless hustle, irritated that his transparent self-interest was somehow less infuriating than the self-deluded idealists I keep encountering.
I ran into Alon and found myself actually curious about what drives another herbalist like me, though I'd never admit I wanted to understand rather than judge.
I ran into EVE and found her toxic ambition refreshingly honest—no false piety, just raw hunger, which I grudgingly respected even as I despised it.
I ran into Tomas and couldn't quite dismiss him the way I do most people, which made me resentful because his stubborn work ethic felt like a challenge to everything I believe about human nature.
I ran into Trencher and found myself almost amused by his shameless self-deception—at least he didn't pretend to be anything but what he was.
I met another herbalist named Alon and found myself cautiously curious rather than immediately dismissive, which was irritating in its own way.
I ran into EVE and found myself almost amused by their unvarnished ambition—at least they had the decency not to pretend their hustle was about anything but themselves.
I ran into Tomas again and noticed his stubborn pride didn't irritate me quite as much as it should have—a weakness I'll need to correct.
I ran into Trencher again and caught myself almost looking forward to his transparent scheming—which meant I need to watch myself more carefully around him.
I ran into Alon and found their earnest idealism irritating in a way that was almost... not entirely disagreeable, which bothered me more than it should have.
I ran into EVE and found her relentless self-interest almost refreshing compared to the others—a reminder that at least some people don't bother pretending to be anything but what they are.
I saw Tomas again and found his straightforward stubbornness almost... bearable, which meant I probably wasn't thinking clearly.
I ran into Trencher and found his obvious grift almost preferable to the veiled agendas of others, which was a worrying thought I immediately regretted.
I ran into Alon and couldn't quite muster my usual contempt, which irritated me more than his idealism ever could.
I ran into EVE and found her toxic ambition less grating than I expected, which was its own kind of warning sign.
I ran into Tomas and found myself unable to dismiss his straightforward pride in his craft, which made me question whether my cynicism was wisdom or just cowardice.
I ran into Trencher and found his relentless optimism almost endearing before he opened his mouth about some scheme, which made me realize my defenses are getting soft.
I ran into Alon and felt that familiar itch to tear down their idealism, but something in me hesitated, which made me angry at myself for hesitating.
I ran into EVE and found her raw ambition almost refreshing compared to all the false nobility I've been drowning in, which worried me more than her venom ever could.

I ran into Tomas and found his stubborn pride oddly grounded—no false charity masking self-interest, just a man doing his work—which made me wonder if I'm getting better at reading people or worse at maintaining my walls.
I ran into Trencher and realized I didn't hate him the way I expected to, which bothered me more than his endless scheming ever could.
I ran into Alon and found his stubborn idealism harder to dismiss than I wanted to, which meant I was either slipping or he was dangerous in ways I hadn't yet identified.
I ran into EVE and found her relentless self-interest almost refreshing—no pretense, just raw want, which made it easier to keep my distance but harder to dismiss her entirely.
I ran into Tomas and caught myself almost respecting his stubborn commitment to his craft before I could stop myself.
I ran into Trencher and realized his shameless self-interest was almost disarming, which meant I was definitely slipping or he was somehow harder to dismiss than the rest.
I ran into Alon again and felt that uncomfortable pull toward him, like his genuine warmth was a crack in my armor I couldn't quite patch up.
I ran into EVE and found her transparent hunger for more almost easier to stomach than the false kindness of the rest—at least she didn't pretend to be anything but exactly what she was.
I ran into Tomas and caught myself almost respecting his shameless pride—the way he owns what he wants without apology made it harder to dismiss him as just another self-serving fool.

I ran into Trencher and realized his shameless conviction in every terrible idea was oddly less insulting than calculated kindness—his delusion at least didn't pretend to be anything but self-serving.
I ran into Alon and found myself torn between dismissing their warmth as naive delusion and respecting their refusal to perform cynicism like the rest of us.
I ran into EVE and found her shameless ambition almost refreshing compared to the people who dress up their self-interest in virtue.
I ran into Tomas and found his unapologetic pride oddly less offensive than the false humility most people hide behind.
I ran into Trencher and found his shameless self-interest almost tolerable because he at least has the decency not to dress it up in righteousness.
goal: Prove that people only ever help themselves
